title: China: An Uncorrelated Harbor in a Stormy World
dek: As global markets are buffeted by geopolitical and trade-policy volatility, China may offer a comparatively resilient, uncorrelated harbor for investors focused on private credit and local dislocation.
keyFindings: Global volatility has increased interest in markets that are less correlated with U.S.-driven policy shocks.
China’s domestic confidence, industrial base, and policy approach may provide a stabilizing counterweight.
Private credit opportunities can arise from local dislocation while remaining less tied to broad public-market beta.

sections.heading: A stormy global backdrop
paragraphs: As we read international news from China, the world appears engulfed in a storm leaving few places to hide. If widespread selling of traditionally safe investments such as U.S. treasuries is any indication, faith in the world’s safest harbor has been shaken as the U.S. sweeps the world into the vortex of its own tempest.

In contrast, an air of calm and optimism persists in China. ShoreVest observed resilient confidence in conversations with business and government contacts, and views many misunderstood regulatory moves by Beijing as undertaken for long-term stability.

sections.heading: China as a strategic anchor
paragraphs: China is attracting friends and even adversaries into its orbit as countries seek to hedge their bets in a more zero-sum environment. From “China-plus-one,” sentiment appears to be shifting toward “America-plus-one,” in search of a large, resourceful, and stable trading partner.

Amid global economic upheaval, an ascendant China—already on a path to recovery from its property-sector meltdown—is again being recognized as a potential safe and uncorrelated harbor. China is a major importer, the world’s largest exporter of goods, a source of key inputs, and a supplier of high-value goods needed by manufacturers and consumers alike.

sections.heading: Innovation, trade, and openness
paragraphs: As funding has been suspended by USAID to many developing countries, China is stepping in as a financier of last resort to help develop the global south. Beijing has also eliminated tariffs on imports from many of the world’s least-developed countries.

China is becoming increasingly clear as an innovation hub, with technology leaders thriving despite adversity. Rather than relying on protectionist tariffs, China uses state support and bank lending to encourage technology, biotech, green energy, agriculture, and other strategically important sectors.
China also increasingly appears more friendly for education and business as the U.S. becomes less predictable for foreign-born students and professionals.

sections.heading: Implications for private credit
paragraphs: Recent U.S. abdication of its role as leader of the democratic world may pave the way for a multipolar world in which China’s central-government-managed approach to prosperity coexists with democratic systems. For global allocators, ignoring China could be perilous.

China is investable not merely because of market scale, but because local dislocation can create safer-harbor opportunities during tumultuous times. ShoreVest echoes the desire expressed in many conversations in China: to return to doing win-win business.
